SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper gives a concrete model (TransE-style joint embedding with a margin loss (Eq.1–3) and a rating constraint (Eq.4–5)) and reports empirical gains (e.g., ~9% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvements in the hotel domain). However, training details, hyperparameter choices (other than θ mentioned), and statistical significance of reported gains are not provided, leaving some experimental claims weakly supported. The core modelling assumptions (treating reviewer as a translation between product and review embedding) are plausible but not theoretically justified in the text, representing an unsupported modeling assumption.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The approach combines existing components — TransE-like graph embedding and CNN-based text encoding — to tackle the cold-start review spam problem; the idea of jointly embedding textual and behavioral signals for this task is a reasonable contribution but largely a composition of known techniques. The paper cites related prior work (e.g., Wang et al. (2016), Mukherjee et al.) and does not present a fundamentally new modelling paradigm, so the novelty is moderate (an incremental but application-relevant extension).

CLARITY.justification: The paper contains many repeated and fragmented sentences, duplicated citations, and formatting issues which hinder readability (numerous repeated blocks and citation fragments throughout the text). While equations and the high-level model are presented, notation and exposition are sometimes messy (e.g., inconsistent spacing and repeated paragraphs), making parts of the method and experimental setup harder to follow.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments use a real-world Yelp split (train before 2012, test new reviewers after 2012) and compare multiple baselines (SVM with bigrams, behavioral features, CNN-only, and intuitive similarity approximations), and report domain-specific results (hotel vs restaurant). However, the paper lacks important analyses such as thorough ablations (e.g., sensitivity to θ, embedding dimensions), failure modes, reproducibility details (training hyperparameters, dataset splits specifics), and statistical tests, limiting the experimental depth.

IMPACT.justification: Detecting review spam at cold-start (immediately after a first review) addresses a practically important problem and could be useful for online platforms, as the paper motivates with business impact statistics. Nevertheless, the technique is an incremental combination of existing methods and the reported evaluation—while promising—does not convincingly demonstrate transformative benefits across broader settings, so the likely impact is moderate.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (joint text+behavior embeddings for spam detection) fits well within NLP/ML venues that accept work on representation learning and applied detection tasks. The methodology (CNNs, embedding losses, TransE-style graph modeling) and evaluation on a real dataset align with standards expected at top NLP/ML conferences, making it an appropriate submission with minor concerns about presentation and experimental completeness.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to several relevant baselines including SVM with linguistic (bigrams) and behavioral features, an independent supervised CNN, and intuitive similarity-based approximations (edit-distance and W2V average), and cites prior influential works (Mukherjee et al., Rayana and Akoglu). While these comparisons are relevant and performed on the same dataset/metrics, the paper does not report statistical significance or compare to some other recent graph/embedding approaches in depth, so a few omissions remain.
